sentences.text: Well, Poland was devastated by the war, naturally.
And we saw a great deal of ruins.
The only place that did survive the war was the city of Krakow miraculously did escape.
Also, in the conversation with the Germans, I was asking them about trains that were discovered in railroad stations and when the door opened, corpses fell out of those wagons.
And I said, what-- what is that?
And the answer that the Germans gave me, they said, well, we arrest and put in concentration camps Communists and-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] and those who are traitors.
But they didn't mention that innocent people were there.
And they say, if you heard that-- this-- when I objected against this, they said, well, the Allied propaganda is inventing this, they say.
And this is not true that we have innocent people.
Only because I cited to them the case of the Archbishop of Vienna, who protested against the German occupation, who was sent to Dachau.
And I say, this is-- he was not a Communist.
He was not a traitor either.
He was just an Austrian.
And well, they say, we don't know about it.
So-- but the amazing thing was-- when I arrived in Berlin was that everything was rationed there.
And I arrived just before Christmas '41, I arrived there.
And when I checked into the hotel, they asked me whether you would like to have some ersatz coffee and some bread.
I said, ooh, I would love to after a long journey.
Where is your ration card?
I said, I don't have any ration card.
Well, you better hurry up, because this is just before Christmas.
You have to go get a ration card.
So I ran to the office that they pointed out and the office was closed.
And a German officer was passing by.
And he says, what's the matter?
I said, well, I just arrived.
I don't have a ration card.
He says, wait a minute.
I know one place-- follow me-- where you could get a ration card.
So I followed him.
The place was closed.
He says, well, so sorry.
But why don't you contact your diplomatic representative?
I say, oh, that's a good idea.
So I found the address of the Spanish consul.
And I ran over to his apartment to-- I mean, where he lived.
And when I arrived there, they say, well, just an hour ago the consul left for Christmas vacation to Spain.
So sorry.
Why don't you come after Christmas?
So no ration card.
I was very hungry.
And I couldn't get anything without a ration card.
Next day I walked around Berlin.
I saw the Reich Chancellery in Potsdamer Platz.
I saw a whole crowd surrounding a civilian.
And I said, what's going on?
Oh, he's a guide and he will show us Berlin.
I had some German money, so I say, OK.
Let's see Berlin.
And I was amazed that Berlin was not bombed.
I mean, there was only a few roofs were burned, but otherwise Berlin was intact.
We were able to see the Reich Chancellery and everything.
And we paid only, I think, five marks for the whole thing.
And then I approached the man and I say, I'm hungry and I don't have any ration card.
He says, well, there is a restaurant in Friedrichstrasse where they do serve some vegetables which is not on the ration.
You can have that.
So I went there and I ate that, even if I didn't particularly like it, but it was some food for Christmas.
After Christmas I went to the Spanish embassy and they gave me a whole pile of ration cards and I had a feast.
The Germans were looking at me saying, he's crazy.
He's using a ration card for several months.
But I had a big meal.
The only thing that I was amazed in the German restaurant is that they had people in German uniforms but not speaking German.
I saw a group of French who were in German uniforms with a French flag.
And they were volunteers going to the Eastern Front.
And then at another table, I saw some Dutch who were talking Dutch.
And they had also the uniform, German uniform and the flag.
Only the waiter spoke German.
Otherwise they were all different languages there, people going to the front.
In fact, when I arrived in Spain, the-- there was a Spanish colonel who said, oh, we need you as an interpreter.
We're sending a division to the Eastern Front.
It was the Blue Division.
We need an interpreter.
Would you volunteer?
I say, no.
I have to join my mother.
I can't do that.
And so-- and they say, well, we will wait until you are 21.
According to Spanish law, we can draft you at 21.
Oh boy, was I praying to get the American visa, because they wanted me to go as an interpreter with the Blue Division.
And that Blue Division did suffer.
It was under-- General Munoz Grandes was the commander of that Blue Division.
When I was in Spain in '91 delivering a paper, I went to the museum and I saw those Spanish and German uniform there.
And they say, this room is now being closed.
You are the last one to see, because the Prime Minister of Spain is a Socialist.
At that time it was Gonzales.
Even if they have a king, they-- they need to have-- but now, he lost the election just recently.
But it was interesting to talk to the officers on the train.
They were telling about how the railroads were blown up.
And they were no use to the German railroad cars, because the German railroad cars are of European width, while the railroad-- there are only two countries in Europe that have a wide-gauge railroad.
And that would be Soviet Union and Spain.
Because in Spain, I also had to get off the French train and walk to the Spanish train.
Well, the Germans couldn't do that, so they had lot of delays trying to get the gauge for their train to roll.
And they were complaining already at that time.
In 1941, they were complaining about the partisans that were quite active, attacking [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] any stray German.
And they did hang a lot of people, but they were not-- they-- they said that they didn't know anything about the atrocities.
Because I listened to BBC, I knew that there were some atrocities and they would deny that completely.
And I know in our German school we didn't know about that either.
I mean, you know, it was kept strictly secret about the atrocities that they have committed, putting people in concentration camps.
And I-- I didn't see any concentration camps when I was traveling through Germany.
The train went through Breslau and so on.
I-- I didn't see that.
The only thing is that in France, the water was not safe.
I was thirsty and I ran right away to drink water.
And the German major yelled, don't touch the water.
I say, why?
He says, it's polluted.
You are going to get sick.
I said, but I'm thirsty.
Well, in France, what you do, you buy some Bordeaux wine.
And it was not very expensive.
I drank half a bottle and fell asleep.
I slept from Paris to Orleans.
[LAUGHS] That was the only-- the only thing that you could drink.
Well, even [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] after the war water was-- in many places was not safe.
We were issued some pills to put it in the water, you know, so on.
They had to repair the water system from the bombings and so on.
So we had very, very interesting conversations with the-- with the Germans.
They didn't know really who I am.
[LAUGHS]
That was in 1945, so I guess I was 24.
And I was told that they are looking for linguists to send to-- to Nuremberg, would I apply for it?
And I applied.
And they didn't believe that I speak all those languages, so they sent me to the State Department to take an oral exam in each one of the language, and a written exam.
And then I was hired to go to Nuremberg, to the Nuremberg trial.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I had to stay at Fort Totten 'til airplane took off and we flew to Goose Bay, Labrador.
And then from there, we flew-- we were supposed to stop at London, and they didn't stop there, but they stopped in Paris.
And then from Paris, we flew into Germany.
No.
I knew that they needed some language specialists.
And so I imagined that most probably there would be some translation work to be done.
And later on, I found out that there were about 20,000 Americans working at Nuremberg, so there were quite a few Americans in Nuremberg.
Well, Mother was quite happy that I go to Nuremberg.
She says, justice has to be done, because war criminals have not been prosecuted after World War I, so something has to be done.
The idea of Nuremberg was-- just like President Wilson used to say that World War I is to stop all other wars, we were hoping that war crimes would stop after Nuremberg.
And we were hoping that this would be realized.
But now I'm awfully disappointed.
War crimes are being committed right and left.
They didn't learn any lesson from-- from Nuremberg.
Well, I was put right away into a hotel that was still being repaired, right next to the railroad station.
And I was well taken care of.
Later on I got some quarters in a nearby village, to be with other young Americans.
It was-- so it was very friendly, very friendly, very efficient.
We had wonderful food, much better than it was in the Army.
[LAUGHS] We had those fancy restaurants.
Naturally, the dishes that they had, the silverware had a stamp of Reichsparteitag, Nuremberg, the city of the Party, or the Nazi Party.
Well, the first meal that I had there, all the forks and knives disappeared.
Everybody took a souvenir.
I still have a fork with Reichsparteitag at home as a souvenir.
And the lady next to me was [INAUDIBLE] says, take mine.
[LAUGHS] You have a pocket.
Later on we were served, naturally, plastic dishes.
[LAUGHS] Silver, the silverware was going as souvenir.
Even we had some napkins with the Nazi insignia.
That disappeared, too.
We got paper napkins after it.
Everybody was taking souvenirs.
[LAUGHS] I guess that's one of our national sports, right, take some souvenirs.
[LAUGHS]
They were very interesting.
They were very interesting.
Most of them were discharged from the army.
There were some army personnel too, like Colonel Dostert.
He was in charge of the interpreters.
And he was the one that introduced that simultaneous translation that is being used at the UN.
He introduced that, where you had a dial and you could have simultaneous translation.

That is a contribution to Nuremberg trial, to our country here, because I understand Georgetown University is using the same method too, you know, with-- which Colonel Dostert introduced in Georgetown after the Nuremberg trial.
So that was a gift of the Nuremberg trial to the United States.
And we had very nice quarters and were very well taken care of.
And the only thing is that [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] in the beginning, it was very hard to read those documents that we captured.
It was very hard to read those documents, because the Germans are very thorough and they go into details.
And they were not-- they didn't take any effort to destroy the evidence.
So the evidence was there, snapshots, everything described in detail.
Some of those crimes are not even mentioned today, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] like for example, making soap out of human bodies, or lampshades were made.
And they went into detail describing everything.
And even their testimony-- some people were even proud of committing horrible war crimes, like the commander of Auschwitz.
I saw him for two days.
And he was so proud that he was using a more efficient gas, Cyclotron C gas, that would kill people quicker than in Treblinka that was not far away.
And he was so proud that he camouflaged his camp, 'arbeit macht frei,' you know?
Work makes you free.
And he mentioned himself that children were involved, women were involved.
And he says, well, we didn't waste anything.
He says, everything that they had to take off had to be put according to size in a pile.
And if they did have any jewelry or anything, we gave them a receipt and took it away.
And they had big signs that you have to take showers before you are resettled in the Eastern Territories.
That was a story.
So all women, children, and men, they were walked towards the gas chambers, which were camouflaged as shower rooms.
And then they were locked hermetically in those.
And he says, well, before they were locked in those rooms, we cut their hair because the hair had to be used for mattresses.
And we knew that they were dead after the cries stopped.
And then they had a detail of some inmates who would open the mouth of every victim and take their gold teeth out, put it in special sacks, which they sent to the Reichsbank in Germany.
In fact, when we occupied Germany we found many, many of these sacks with gold teeth that were taken from the victims.
Then the victims would be taken to the gas chamber.
And he says, the bones would not burn, so they had a special machine for grinding bones.
And the ashes and what was the grinding bones were used as fertilizer.
And they had even pictures taken of huge cabbages and lettuce grown on this.
When they were making soap, they had to boil the bodies, so they would stack up the hands as a pyramid.
And you had blood dripping and an SS man smiling to the camera with his Luger pistol, and-- because the head cannot be used for making soap.
And the corpses were then boiled and soap was made.
And then the skeletons would be sent to universities and schools in Germany to study anatomy.
They were very proud of that.
And they went into gory details describing all-- all this.
In fact, Frank, who was the governor of occupied Poland-- he told us about 10-volume personal diary that he buried under a tree in Poland.
We went there and we found the best evidence against him, which he could not deny because he told us where it was hidden.
It was really unusual.
Some, like a lawyer, Kaltenbrunner, for example, he told us that he doesn't recognize his own signature on the documents that he signed.
But he didn't get away with that.
I know some of the militaries thought that the military should not be tried by war crimes.
Like for example, we tried Keitel, Oberkommando der Wehrmacht, and Jodl, and the two admirals, Doenitz and Raeder.
And even some American military said, well, they had to follow orders, so it's not their fault.
Well, we found out that von Brauchitsch, who was a famous German general, when the invasion of Poland happened, he distinguished himself.
And he was promoted to be the Chief of Staff of the German Army.
And then Hitler decided to start the war against Soviet Union, and Brauchitsch protested.
He says, you are crazy.
You can't fight on two fronts.
Bismarck has said, Germany doesn't have the resources.
So Hitler didn't kill him, but he demoted him to the command of an army, and he was not punished for disagreeing.
So we are told, well, the other officers could do the same thing.
They liked their job too much.
Plus, there was a secret document called Nacht und Nebel, Night and Fog.
And this document was about 50 American and British flyers who were told to escape the prisoner war camp in order to get back on duty.
And they escaped several times and they were re-captured and brought back.
And Himmler, the chief of the Gestapo, said they have to be taught a lesson.
And he asked Keitel to sign this document, Night and Fog, by transferring these flyers to Gestapo.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And these flyers were executed.
Germany signed the Geneva Convention, so therefore Keitel was-- knew what he was doing.
He was sentencing PWs to their death.
Plus, we had PWs, American PWs, in those concentration camp.
Like Ravensbruck and Buchenwald, there were American prisoners of war.
We had a lieutenant commander of the Navy who said that if I would not have been freed today, I would be dead.
And he had a whole bunch of dog tags.
These people didn't make it.
PWs killed in them, and that is not mentioned, that PWs were killed there, too.
And 6 million Poles were killed, too, Gypsies.
But the PWs were killed, too.
Only-- our PWs were killed, this-- this example.
There were even some Navy people too that were transferred to Gestapo and killed.
So it-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it was quite emotional to see how people suffered in-- in those concentration camps.
They did conduct human experiments too, and that was horrible.
Children were afraid to get into an ambulance, because we ran out of transportation, and we thought, well, we will transport them-- because Hitler kidnapped children who had blue eyes and were blond.
And the mothers and the families were looking for those children, from Yugoslavia, from Romania, from other parts.
And we wanted to transport them in those ambulances, and they refused.
They start crying, you know.
Then we found out that the Germans had a special gas chamber installed in those ambulances and killing children.
So no wonder they were afraid to get in the ambulance.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
So one trial that was stopped in the middle was the trial against Krupp, Krupp von Bohlen, who was a-- a big industrialist in Germany, and he was using slave labor.
And the SS was kidnapping young people in occupied territories and selling them to the industrialists as slaves to work in their factories.
And there were two girls that were from the Ukraine.
And these two girls were kidnapped when they were 14.
And they didn't even finish school, and so they spoke only the Ukrainian dialect.
And I was pulled from my work, because I know the Ukrainian dialect, to interpret during the court proceedings.
And you have the photograph.
And it was awful under what conditions they were.
They were living in a barracks.
The only food that they had was soup made out of potato peels.
And they had to work in those steel factories where it's awfully hot.
And one girl was pregnant, and she passed out because of that heat.
She was not given any medical assistance.
She gave birth right there and died.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] when the others workers help her, they say ah, that Russian swine can die.
It's all right.
Because the slave laborer who were not Jewish, they had a little square and it was written there O-S-T.
That means Eastern slave.
And my uncle was among those slaves too, where the American government and the British government was turning these slaves back to Stalin to be executed.
And my uncle heard those rumors and he fled from the refugee camp and hid in the forest.
Next day, American MPs came in and dragged the Russians into buses to take them to the Communist Soviet Union lines.
And some of them broke the window and cut their wrists to commit suicide.
Our medics fixed them up.
Well, after my uncle saw that, he fled to Munich and found a German family that took him in.
And he somehow managed to make an announcement in the newspaper in New York.
My mother read that.
And she right away called me at Nuremberg and say, listen, if you have time, your uncle that you have never seen with his wife and child, they are at this address, living in a private German home.
So I took a weekend off and I went to see my uncle for the first time.
And he was telling me what happened to him.
He was in the same regiment of my father, fighting the Civil War, and he was sent for reconnaissance.
And he was cut off, because you know, when civil war happens, the lines are not well established.
And my father couldn't find him, had to retreat under the pressure.
And my uncle and his men hid in some mines near Odessa there.
And they were hiding during the day.
And during the night, they would try to find some food.
And once one of the soldiers that was serving under my uncle said, look, I got a newspaper.
The Civil War is over, so we can go home.
Well, my uncle said, I don't have any authority to stop you.
Sure, you can go home.
But you might be executed because you fought against the Communists.
And oh, no, no.
The war is over.
Well, all of them were executed.
My uncle kept hiding.
And one night he found a cute girl that was walking down the beach.
She was the daughter of a union man, labor union.
And they fell in love.
But my uncle refused to come out.
He says, I don't want to be executed.
She wanted to get married.
And she talked-- she talked to her-- to her father.
Her father represented the case.
And so the Party asked him, what is his profession?
Said, he's an engineer.
Just at that time, Stalin killed all the engineers, and they didn't have any engineers.
They got some Americans.
And so he got a job to build the Dnieper dam.
We're going to have to pause here to change tape.
Sure.
